questions.text: How many MPI functions are considered sufficient for programming a distributed system memory machine?
questions.explanation: According to the material, six MPI functions are sufficient for programming a distributed system memory machine.

questions.text: In MPI point-to-point communication, which description best characterizes the interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One-sided communication where only the sender is active

choices.label: B
choices.text: Collective communication involving all processes in a communicator

choices.label: C
choices.text: Two-sided communication where both processes actively participate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Broadcasting data from a root process to all other processes

questions.explanation: Point-to-point operations involve data exchange between two processes where both are actively participating, known as two-sided communication.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT part of the message 'envelope' or header in MPI?
questions.explanation: The beginning of the buffer is part of the data description, not the message header (envelope).

questions.text: What does the MPI 'Non-overtaking' rule guarantee?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Messages from process A to process B arrive in the same order they were posted

choices.label: B
choices.text: The receiver process cannot finish before the sender

choices.label: D
choices.text: A message is always received by the first available Recv operation

questions.explanation: The non-overtaking rule guarantees that if process A sends two messages to process B, they arrive in the same order they were sent.

questions.text: According to the message-based paradigm in MPI, a single message can be received by how many `Recv` operations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As many as requested by the communicator

questions.explanation: MPI specifies that a single message cannot be received with more than one Recv operation.

questions.text: Which of these parameters is NOT used by the receiver to match an incoming message?
questions.explanation: Message length is not used for matching. Matching is based on sender, tag, and communicator.

questions.text: What occurs if a received message is longer than the expected message length provided in the `Recv` operation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The message is truncated automatically without error

choices.label: C
choices.text: The extra data is stored in a temporary system buffer

choices.label: D
choices.text: The operation waits for more buffer space to become available

questions.explanation: If the received message is longer than the expected message, MPI returns an error code (MPI_ERR_TRUNC), or the application may abort/deadlock.

questions.text: Which piece of information is directly accessible from the MPI status object in C?
questions.explanation: The rank of the sender, the tag, and the error code are directly accessible from the status object. Message length and cancellation status are indirectly accessible through function calls.

questions.text: If a programmer does not need the status information from `MPI_Recv`, what should they pass as the status argument?
questions.explanation: If you are not interested in the status, you can pass MPI_STATUS_NULL.

questions.text: When does a regular `MPI_Send` operation return?

choices.label: B
choices.text: When the data is safely stored away

choices.label: C
choices.text: When the receiver process starts receiving the data

choices.label: D
choices.text: When the entire ring communication is complete

questions.explanation: A regular (blocking) MPI_Send returns when the data is safely stored away (either in a system buffer or delivered to the receiver).

questions.text: Which prefix is used for MPI functions that initiate non-blocking communication?
questions.explanation: Immediate (non-blocking) functions have an 'I' prefix, such as MPI_Isend or MPI_Irecv.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the 'request' argument in non-blocking MPI functions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To request more memory from the system

choices.label: B
choices.text: To uniquely identify an ongoing communication to check for completion later

choices.label: C
choices.text: To specify the priority of the message

choices.label: D
choices.text: To ask the receiver if they are ready

questions.explanation: A request uniquely identifies an ongoing communication and is used to check or wait for the completion of a posted communication.

questions.text: Is it permissible to modify the communication buffer immediately after calling `MPI_Isend`?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the data is already copied to the system

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only for the first half of the buffer

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, it is not allowed to touch the buffer until completion

questions.explanation: After initiating a non-blocking communication, it is not allowed to modify the buffer until completion because you cannot assume when the message will actually be transferred.

questions.text: Which function would you use to block execution until a specific non-blocking communication has finished?
questions.explanation: MPI_Wait is used to wait for the completion of a non-blocking operation.

questions.text: What is the difference between `MPI_Wait` and `MPI_Test`?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MPI_Wait is for sends, MPI_Test is for receives

choices.label: B
choices.text: MPI_Wait blocks until completion; MPI_Test returns immediately with a flag

choices.label: C
choices.text: MPI_Wait is only for collective operations

questions.explanation: Wait-functions block until the communication is finished, while Test-functions check if a communication has finished and return immediately.

questions.text: Which function checks if ALL communications in a list of requests have finished?
questions.explanation: MPI_Testall checks whether all communications in a provided list have finished.

questions.text: Information regarding whether an MPI message has been cancelled is accessible through:
questions.explanation: Information such as message length and whether it has been cancelled is accessible indirectly through function calls using the status object.

questions.text: In the context of the ring implementation example, which process typically starts the communication?
questions.explanation: In the example provided, Rank 0 starts the ring implementation.

questions.text: What happens if a received message is shorter than the expected buffer size?

choices.label: C
choices.text: No problem occurs; the message is received normally

choices.label: D
choices.text: The remaining buffer space is filled with random data

questions.explanation: If the received message is shorter than the expected message, there are no problems.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a completion function for non-blocking operations?
questions.explanation: MPI_Recv_check is not a standard completion function mentioned; Wait and Test variants (all, any, some) are the standard ones.

questions.text: MPI guarantees reliability in point-to-point communication. This means:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Messages are guaranteed to arrive within a specific time

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sender will always know when the receiver has read the data

questions.explanation: Reliability in MPI guarantees that no message gets lost during the communication process.

questions.text: Which function allows a program to check how many communications from a list have finished?
questions.explanation: MPI_Testsome checks how many of a list of communications have finished.

questions.text: In C, which field of the `MPI_Status` structure stores the rank of the process that sent the message?
questions.explanation: In the standard C implementation of MPI, the sender's rank is stored in the `MPI_SOURCE` field of the status object.

questions.text: If you are using a list of requests, which function will block until at least one of the requests is completed?
questions.explanation: MPI_Waitany blocks until any one of the communications in a list has finished.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the data being sent in an MPI message?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Buffer beginning, data-type, and number of elements

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sender rank, receiver rank, and tag

questions.explanation: The data part of a message is described by the beginning of the buffer, the data-type, and the number of elements.

questions.text: What is a potential consequence of an `MPI_ERR_TRUNC` error?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The application will automatically retry with a larger buffer

choices.label: B
choices.text: The application might deadlock or abort

choices.label: C
choices.text: The extra data is lost but the program continues safely

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sender is notified to resend a shorter message

questions.explanation: When a truncation error occurs, the application might abort, deadlock, or write a core-dump.

questions.text: Why are non-blocking operations often used in complex MPI programs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To reduce the number of processes needed

choices.label: B
choices.text: To avoid deadlocks and overlap communication with computation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because they are simpler to implement than Send/Recv

choices.label: D
choices.text: To ensure messages are never lost

questions.explanation: Non-blocking operations allow the program to continue executing while communication is ongoing, which helps in avoiding deadlocks (as revisited in the text) and improving performance.

questions.text: Which function is used to check if a specific communication has finished without blocking?
questions.explanation: MPI_Test checks whether a communication has finished and returns a flag immediately.

questions.text: In MPI, a 'tag' is used for:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Providing an additional level of message matching

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specifying the size of the buffer

questions.explanation: The tag is part of the message envelope and must match the expected tag at the receiver for the message to be matched.

questions.text: Which information is considered 'indirectly accessible' through function calls using the status object?
questions.explanation: The length of the message and whether it was cancelled are indirectly accessible through function calls like `MPI_Get_count`.
